title: Managing Marek's disease in a backyard flock
subtitle: What Marek's looks like, how the vaccine works, and what to do once it arrives.
excerpt: Marek's disease is present in most UK chicken flocks and cannot be eradicated from an established holding. The herpesvirus that causes it spreads through feather dander and is extremely persistent in the environment. Vaccination at day old is the standard prevention; understanding what vaccinated birds can and cannot do shapes how you manage a flock going forward.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens (2018). Witter (1998), Poultry Science: summary of Marek's disease epidemiology in commercial and backyard flocks. Merck Veterinary Manual: Marek's Disease, merckvetmanual.com.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herpesvirus infection of chickens that causes lymphoma formation in the peripheral nerves, viscera, eyes, and skin. Commonly presents as progressive leg or wing paralysis in young birds aged 6 to 30 weeks. Some birds develop internal tumours with no external signs. The virus spreads via feather dander; it is extremely stable in the environment.

A day-old vaccination that protects against clinical disease but does not prevent infection or viral shedding. Vaccinated birds can carry and shed the virus without becoming ill themselves, but unvaccinated birds in contact with vaccinated ones remain at risk of developing Marek's disease.

A form of Marek's disease causing internal tumour formation in the liver, spleen, kidneys, and ovaries, with few external signs until the bird collapses. Often confused with other causes of sudden death. More common in highly susceptible genetic lines.

content.text: Marek's disease is one of the most widespread diseases in domestic poultry worldwide. On most established UK smallholdings, the virus is already present in the ground and the housing, shed by birds over years.
itself cannot be treated once a bird develops clinical signs. Management is about minimising losses through vaccination of new stock and making informed decisions about the flock's composition.

The classic presentation is a young bird aged 8 to 20 weeks developing a progressive leg weakness, one leg extended forward and one backward in the typical 'splits' posture. This is peripheral nerve lymphoma affecting the sciatic nerve. The bird remains bright and alert initially but cannot reach food or water without help. Without supportive care it will die from dehydration or from being outcompeted by flock mates.
Some birds develop grey iris changes (ocular Marek's) or lumpy skin growths. Others die suddenly from internal tumours with no obvious prior signs: this is
How the vaccine works and its limits

is given subcutaneously at day old, typically by the hatchery before chicks leave. It stimulates immunity against clinical disease but does not prevent infection. A vaccinated bird exposed to the virus will carry it and shed it to other birds. This is sometimes called a 'leaky' vaccine. The practical consequence: vaccinated birds on an infected property do not become ill, but any unvaccinated birds on the same holding remain at risk.
Buying vaccinated chicks' and sourcing new stock

Always purchase point-of-lay hens and chicks from a source that vaccinates at day old. Ask the supplier explicitly; not all smallholder breeders vaccinate. Unvaccinated chicks placed on a holding where Marek's is established will frequently develop disease within weeks.

Heritage breed lines and show lines have lower innate resistance to Marek's than commercial hybrids in many cases. If you keep heritage breeds and experience high losses, ask your vet about the HVT plus SB-1 dual vaccine protocol, which provides broader protection than single-strain vaccines.

There is no treatment for Marek's. A bird showing leg paralysis can be supported in a comfortable pen with food and water placed within easy reach to assess whether it maintains weight. Some birds stabilise and live in reasonable comfort for months; others deteriorate rapidly. The welfare check is straightforward: a bird that cannot reach food or water, is losing weight, or is being attacked by pen mates is not a candidate for extended support.

Marek's disease is not a notifiable disease in the UK. You do not need to report it to APHA. However, if you are experiencing unexplained mortality in young birds, it is worth contacting your vet for a postmortem on one or two carcasses: the differential diagnoses include Marek's but also Lymphoid leucosis, which is an entirely separate condition with different biosecurity implications.
